quiz_questions.question_text: What is polarization?

options.text: Restricting oscillation of a transverse wave to a single plane
Increasing the speed of light
Bending light around obstacles
Changing the color of light

quiz_questions.explanation: Polarization restricts the oscillation of a transverse wave to a single plane. Only transverse waves can be polarized.

quiz_questions.question_text: Can longitudinal waves be polarized?

options.text: Yes
No — only transverse waves
Only at high frequencies
Only in certain media

quiz_questions.explanation: Longitudinal waves cannot be polarized because their oscillations are already along a single direction (the direction of travel). Only transverse waves, which oscillate perpendicular to travel, can be restricted to one plane.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does polarization prove about light?

options.text: That light has mass
That light is a particle
That light is a transverse wave
That light is longitudinal

quiz_questions.explanation: The fact that light can be polarized proves it is a transverse wave. If light were longitudinal, polarization would be impossible.

quiz_questions.question_text: At θ = 90° (crossed polarizers), what fraction of light passes?
quiz_questions.explanation: By Malus's Law, I = I₀ cos²(90°) = I₀ × 0 = 0. When two polarizers are crossed at 90°, no light passes through the second polarizer.

quiz_questions.question_text: How do polarizing sunglasses work?

options.text: They block all light equally
They block horizontally polarized light (glare)
They change light color
They increase brightness

quiz_questions.explanation: Glare from surfaces like water and roads is mostly horizontally polarized. Polarizing sunglasses have vertically oriented filters that block horizontally polarized light while allowing vertically polarized light through.

quiz_questions.question_text: State Malus's Law.

options.text: I = I₀/cos² θ
I = I₀ cos θ
I = I₀ sin² θ
I = I₀ cos² θ

quiz_questions.explanation: Malus's Law: I = I₀ cos² θ, where I₀ is the intensity of polarized light entering the analyzer and θ is the angle between the polarization direction and the analyzer axis.

quiz_questions.question_text: Name three methods of polarization.

options.text: Gravity, magnetism, friction
Refraction, diffraction, absorption
Filters, reflection, and scattering
Only filters can polarize

quiz_questions.explanation: Light can be polarized by three main methods: absorption (polarizing filters), reflection (Brewster's angle), and scattering (such as sunlight scattered by the atmosphere).

quiz_questions.question_text: What fraction of unpolarized light passes through a single ideal polarizer?

options.text: All of the original light intensity
One half of the original light intensity
One quarter of the original light intensity
None of the original light passes through

quiz_questions.explanation: When unpolarized light passes through a single ideal polarizer, exactly half (50%) of the intensity is transmitted. The polarizer blocks all components not aligned with its transmission axis.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is Brewster's angle?

options.text: The angle at which reflected light is completely polarized
The angle at which all light is absorbed by a surface
The angle at which total internal reflection begins
The angle at which light changes color upon reflection

quiz_questions.explanation: Brewster's angle is the specific angle of incidence at which reflected light becomes completely polarized. At this angle, the reflected and refracted rays are perpendicular to each other (tan θ_B = n₂/n₁).

quiz_questions.question_text: When two polarizers are aligned (θ = 0°), what happens to polarized light?

options.text: No light passes through the second polarizer
Only half the light passes through the second polarizer
All the polarized light passes through the second polarizer
The light becomes unpolarized after the second polarizer

quiz_questions.explanation: By Malus's Law, I = I₀ cos²(0°) = I₀ × 1 = I₀. When the polarization direction is aligned with the analyzer, all polarized light passes through.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'polarizer' in optics?

options.text: A device that increases the intensity of all incoming light beams
A filter that transmits light vibrating in only one specific plane
A lens that focuses parallel light rays to a single focal point
A mirror that reflects light at exactly the same angle it arrived

quiz_questions.explanation: A polarizer is an optical filter that only allows light waves vibrating in one specific plane (the transmission axis) to pass through, blocking all other vibration directions.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an 'analyzer' in polarization experiments?

options.text: The first polarizer that creates polarized light from unpolarized
A second polarizer used to test the polarization of the light
A detector that measures the wavelength of the incoming light
A prism that separates polarized light into different colors

quiz_questions.explanation: An analyzer is a second polarizing filter placed after the first polarizer. By rotating the analyzer and observing intensity changes (Malus's Law), you can determine the polarization state of the light.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'unpolarized light' mean?

options.text: Light that vibrates in all directions perpendicular to its travel
Light that has been filtered to vibrate in a single plane only
Light that can only travel in a straight line without bending
Light that has lost all of its energy and stopped propagating

quiz_questions.explanation: Unpolarized light has electric field vibrations in all directions perpendicular to its direction of travel. Most natural light sources (the sun, light bulbs) produce unpolarized light.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'transmission axis' of a polarizer?

options.text: The direction along which the polarizer allows light to pass through
The direction in which the polarizer blocks all incoming light waves
The physical thickness of the polarizer measured through its center
The angle at which light must arrive for the polarizer to work

quiz_questions.explanation: The transmission axis is the direction along which a polarizer allows the electric field component of light to pass through. Components perpendicular to this axis are blocked.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'glare' refer to in everyday optics?

options.text: Any bright light that comes from an overhead light source directly
Intense reflected light, often horizontally polarized, from flat surfaces
The colored halos seen around bright lights due to diffraction
The dimming of light as it passes through a thick glass window

quiz_questions.explanation: Glare is bright, harsh reflected light, often from flat surfaces like water, roads, or snow. This reflected light is predominantly horizontally polarized, which is why polarizing sunglasses (with vertical filters) effectively reduce it.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'transverse wave' mean?

options.text: A wave where oscillations are parallel to the direction of travel
A wave where oscillations are perpendicular to the direction of travel
A wave that can only travel through solid materials and not fluids
A wave that carries no energy as it moves through a medium

quiz_questions.explanation: A transverse wave has oscillations (vibrations) perpendicular to the direction the wave travels. Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave, which is why it can be polarized.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'intensity' of light?

options.text: The color of light determined by its wavelength value
The power of light per unit area, related to its brightness
The speed at which light travels through a particular medium
The direction in which a light wave is vibrating or oscillating

quiz_questions.explanation: Light intensity is the power (energy per second) delivered per unit area, measured in watts per square meter (W/m²). It corresponds to perceived brightness. Malus's Law describes how intensity changes through a polarizer.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is meant by 'crossed polarizers'?

options.text: Two polarizers placed side by side pointing in the same direction
Two polarizers with transmission axes at 90° to each other
A single polarizer that has been rotated to a 45° angle position
Two polarizers stacked directly on top of each other at 0° angle

quiz_questions.explanation: Crossed polarizers are two polarizing filters with their transmission axes oriented at 90° to each other. No light passes through this combination because cos²(90°) = 0.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is 'scattering' as a method of polarization?

options.text: Light being redirected in many directions by small particles, becoming partially polarized
Light being completely absorbed by dark objects and converted to thermal energy
Light being focused to a single point by a curved mirror or lens surface
Light being split into component colors by passing through a glass prism

quiz_questions.explanation: Scattering occurs when light interacts with small particles (like air molecules). The scattered light becomes partially polarized. This is why the sky appears polarized — it can be observed using polarizing sunglasses.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'polarization by reflection' mean?

options.text: Light reflecting off any surface always becomes completely unpolarized
Light reflected from a surface becomes partially or fully polarized
Only mirrors can polarize light through the reflection of light rays
Reflected light always has a higher intensity than the incoming beam

quiz_questions.explanation: When light reflects off a non-metallic surface, the reflected beam becomes partially polarized. At Brewster's angle, the reflected light is completely polarized with the electric field parallel to the surface.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student looks at a lake on a sunny day and sees a bright glare. They put on polarizing sunglasses and the glare disappears. Why?

options.text: The sunglasses darken all light equally so the glare is less noticeable
The sunglasses block the horizontally polarized reflected glare selectively
The sunglasses change the color of the glare so it blends with surroundings
The sunglasses reflect the glare back toward the lake surface directly

quiz_questions.explanation: Light reflected from a flat water surface is predominantly horizontally polarized. Polarizing sunglasses have vertically oriented transmission axes that block this horizontal polarization, effectively eliminating the glare.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student places two polarizers in a line. When they rotate the second one to 45°, what fraction of the originally polarized light gets through?

options.text: Exactly one quarter of the polarized light intensity
Exactly one half of the polarized light intensity
Exactly three quarters of the polarized light intensity
None of the polarized light passes through at 45 degrees

quiz_questions.explanation: By Malus's Law: I = I₀ cos²(45°) = I₀ × (√2/2)² = I₀ × 0.5. So exactly half the polarized light intensity passes through when the analyzer is at 45°.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student tries to polarize sound waves using a polarizing filter. What happens?

options.text: The sound becomes quieter because the filter blocks half the vibrations
Nothing happens because sound is a longitudinal wave and cannot be polarized
The sound changes pitch because the filter alters its frequency content
The sound becomes polarized and can only travel in one direction after

quiz_questions.explanation: Sound waves are longitudinal — the particles vibrate along the direction of travel. Polarization only works on transverse waves where vibrations can be restricted to a single plane perpendicular to travel.

quiz_questions.question_text: A photographer uses a polarizing filter on their camera while taking a photo of a building with glass windows. What effect does the filter have?

options.text: It makes the windows brighter and more reflective in the photograph
It reduces reflections on the windows so you can see inside the building
It changes the color of the glass windows from clear to dark blue
It has no effect because glass cannot produce polarized light reflections

quiz_questions.explanation: Light reflected off glass windows is partially polarized. A camera polarizing filter oriented to block that polarization direction reduces the reflections, allowing the camera to capture what is behind the glass.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student uses three polarizers in sequence: the first vertical, the second at 45°, and the third horizontal. Does any light pass through all three?

options.text: No, because the first and third are crossed at 90° and block all light
Yes, because the middle polarizer at 45° allows some light to reach the third
Yes, because three polarizers always let all light pass through completely
No, because adding more polarizers always blocks more light progressively

quiz_questions.explanation: Surprisingly, inserting a 45° polarizer between two crossed polarizers allows some light through. The 45° filter rotates the polarization direction, giving a component that can pass through the third filter. The transmitted intensity is I₀ × cos²(45°) × cos²(45°) = I₀/4.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student notices that the sky appears darker through polarizing sunglasses when looking at 90° from the sun. Why?

options.text: The sunglasses tint makes all parts of the sky equally darker overall
Scattered skylight at 90° from the sun is strongly polarized and gets blocked
The atmosphere is thicker when looking at 90° from the sun direction
The sunglasses refract skylight so that less reaches the observer's eyes

quiz_questions.explanation: Sunlight scattered by atmospheric molecules at 90° from the sun is strongly polarized. When polarizing sunglasses are oriented to block this polarization, the sky at that angle appears notably darker.

quiz_questions.question_text: In a lab, a student passes polarized light through an analyzer. As they slowly rotate the analyzer from 0° to 90°, what do they observe?

options.text: The light intensity stays constant throughout the entire rotation
The light gradually dims and reaches zero at 90° rotation angle
The light color changes from red to blue as the angle increases
The light suddenly disappears at exactly 45° rotation angle

quiz_questions.explanation: According to Malus's Law (I = I₀ cos² θ), the intensity decreases smoothly as the angle increases from 0° to 90°. At 0° full intensity passes; at 90° no light passes through.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student learns that LCD screens use polarizers. What role do the polarizers play in displaying images?

options.text: They increase the brightness of the screen backlight to visible levels
They control which areas of the screen appear bright or dark by rotating light
They create the colors displayed on the screen by filtering wavelengths
They protect the screen from scratches and external physical damage

quiz_questions.explanation: LCD screens use two crossed polarizers with a liquid crystal layer between them. The liquid crystals rotate the polarization of light. By controlling this rotation electrically, each pixel can be switched between bright (light passes) and dark (light blocked).

quiz_questions.question_text: A student is fishing and wants to see fish below the water surface. Would polarizing sunglasses help, and why?

options.text: No, polarizing sunglasses only work for blocking direct sunlight beams
Yes, they block the polarized surface glare allowing you to see beneath the water
No, water does not produce any polarized light reflections from its surface
Yes, they magnify the water and make the fish appear larger and closer

quiz_questions.explanation: Light reflected off the water surface creates polarized glare that obscures what is below. Polarizing sunglasses block this horizontally polarized glare, allowing the fisher to see through the surface and spot fish.

quiz_questions.question_text: A student calculates that polarized light passes through an analyzer at 60°. What fraction of the light intensity gets through?

options.text: Three quarters of the initial polarized light intensity
One half of the initial polarized light intensity
One quarter of the initial polarized light intensity
One eighth of the initial polarized light intensity

quiz_questions.explanation: By Malus's Law: I = I₀ cos²(60°) = I₀ × (0.5)² = I₀ × 0.25 = I₀/4. So one quarter (25%) of the polarized light intensity passes through the analyzer at 60°.
